title: Taiwan Defense Budget Deadlocked as US Pressures Legislature to Fund $11.1B Arms Package
summary: Taiwan's Legislative Yuan remains gridlocked over competing defense budget proposals ranging from $12B to $40B, delaying HIMARS payment and asymmetric defense capabilities as US Indo-Pacific Commander demands swift passage.
contents:
## Taiwan Defense Budget Crisis

### Legislative Deadlock
Taiwan's Legislative Yuan faces months of gridlock over competing special defense budget bills. Negotiations between the DPP, KMT, and TPP caucuses on April 23 and 27 ended without consensus on procurement items or total funding.

### Competing Proposals
The Cabinet and DPP proposal totals nearly $40 billion: $11.1 billion for the US arms package approved in December 2025, $20.2 billion for future US purchases, and $9.5 billion for domestic manufacturing. The KMT and TPP propose approximately $12 billion. An internal KMT faction advocates for $25 billion, creating party tensions.

### US Pressure Campaign
US Indo-Pacific Commander Admiral Samuel Paparo and American Institute in Taiwan Director Raymond Greene both called for rapid budget passage. The deadlock has already delayed Taiwan's payment for High Mobility Artillery Rocket Systems (HIMARS), a critical component of the arms package.

### Political Complications
KMT Chairwoman Cheng Li-wun plans a June visit to the United States, stating she intends to alter "Cold War" thinking and improve US views of China. KMT Deputy Chairman Chi Lin-lien criticized LY Speaker Han Kuo-yu for allegedly supporting a larger budget over the party position.

### Capability Gap
The KMT and TPP proposals exclude funding for Taiwanese domestic manufacturing of drones and air defense systems — capabilities analysts consider critical for Taiwan's defense. The next negotiation session convenes May 6.

**Sources**: ISW/AEI China-Taiwan Update
